smartlead_campaign: OR #4 | Ecom Leads | Scroll Stopper | NO Personlization

magnet.report_label: Scroll-Stopper Sheet (the 3 ads we wrote them)

thread.subject: something for H2Ocean
thread.body_text:
hi Edward, just went through your site, not sure how H2Ocean isn't already on my client list haha.

that's not really why I'm here though. I do Meta ads for ecom brands, and rather than pitch you some BS, I just went ahead and built you a few.

built three ad mockups from H2Ocean's actual products, plus a word-class brand playbook I put together from your site and reviews. it's finished and ready to go on my end.

want me to send it your way?

- Kyle

Kyle 

 Sure send me what you are presenting for Meta Ads, we are interested in the creative content.
 

 Thanks
 Eddie

here you go Edward - 2 ad mockups built from H2Ocean's own products, plus your brand playbook. 

 the mockups show the angles, the playbook is the part most brands actually keep - your voice, the words your customers use, and what you can and can't say in ad copy.

 if any of them click, the quickest way to see how I'd actually run them is a 30 min call. → Calendar 

 - Kyle

thread.subject: Re: something for H2Ocean
thread.body_text:
Just set us up for tomorrow. 

 Thanks
 Eddie

copy.tldr: H2Ocean is a 20-year-old natural sea salt mouthwash brand with real dental credibility but zero Meta ads running and no visible reviews - your job is to sell them a careful, proof-first launch that starts small with their in-stock Arctic Ocean Mint product.

deep_dive.what_they_are: A natural oral care brand out of Stuart, Florida, selling for over 20 years. They make a patented sea salt based mouthwash. They lean hard on healing, recovery, and being alcohol-free.
deep_dive.what_they_sell: Two products: Healing Rinse Mouthwash in Arctic Ocean Mint and in Fresh Lemon Ice. Both use Red Sea salt, xylitol, and a natural enzyme. Sold in 8oz, 16oz, and 64oz sizes. Note: the product pages show $17.00 for the mint and $11.00 for the lemon, so pricing runs from $11 up depending on size. I used $11 as the stand-in for their average order value since that is the lowest listed price and the one we were given.
deep_dive.positioning: A premium, natural alternative to harsh alcohol-based mouthwashes. The original patented sea salt rinse. Built for healing after dental work, piercings, and irritation, and gentle enough for daily family use.
deep_dive.who_buys: Health-conscious adults who want a gentle, non-burning rinse. People recovering from dental work, oral surgery, canker sores, or new piercings. Parents wanting something safe for the whole family. Also a real angle with dental professionals, since the site has a whole provider section.
customer_voice: This is great for post-dental work and for oral piercings! It was the only thing that helped my tongue piercing heal. Two thumbs up
strengths: Long track record (20+ years) and a patented formula. That is rare and hard for competitors to copy.
Clear, specific use cases people actively suffer from: canker sores, piercings, post-dental recovery, dry mouth. Pain that people want solved fast.
gaps: No reviews visible anywhere on the site, so there is no social proof for ads to lean on.
No Meta ads found running. They are starting from zero, not fixing something. And the Fresh Lemon Ice product is currently sold out.

game_plan.headline: Launch cold with the Arctic Ocean Mint mouthwash on the strongest healing angle, prove it can sell, then widen and add the second flavor once it is back in stock.

phases.goal: Prove a single ad and a single product can bring in sales at all. No scale talk yet. Just find one thing that works.
campaigns: One testing campaign with the 2 ads from the Scroll-Stopper Sheet, plus one more angle if we have it.
A simple retargeting campaign for people who visit the site but do not buy.
lead_products: Healing Rinse Mouthwash - Arctic Ocean Mint. Two reasons: it is in stock (the Lemon Ice is sold out, so we cannot lead with it), and mint is the safe, familiar flavor most people expect from a mouthwash. Lower risk for a first-time buyer.
phases.creative: Lead with the ad built on the healing and recovery angle, because that is where their real customer quotes point (the tongue piercing review). That is their sharpest, most specific pain. Run it against the 'natural, no alcohol, no burn' angle to see which one people click and buy from.
phases.budget: Start at $100 to $150 a day. Enough to learn, not enough to hurt if it takes time to find the winner.
phases.what_success_looks_like: One ad and one audience that brings in sales steadily at a cost we both feel good about. Even a small, repeatable win here is the goal.

phases.goal: Put more money behind whatever won in month one and widen the audience beyond the first group that responded.
campaigns: Scale the winning ad with more budget.
Test 2 or 3 new audiences around the winning angle (for example, dental recovery, piercing aftercare, natural living).
lead_products: Still Arctic Ocean Mint as the main seller, since it is the proven, in-stock product.
phases.creative: Make 2 or 3 fresh versions of the winning ad so it does not wear out. Start pulling in real customer quotes if Eddie can get us reviews by then.
phases.budget: Raise spend gradually based on results, likely into the $200 to $300 a day range only if the numbers hold.
phases.what_success_looks_like: The winning ad still working at higher spend, and at least one new audience showing promise.

phases.goal: Scale the proven setup and add the second product once it is available.
campaigns: Add the Fresh Lemon Ice as a second product, and test a bundle or subscribe offer if margin allows.
lead_products: Arctic Ocean Mint as the workhorse, plus Healing Rinse Mouthwash - Fresh Lemon Ice added in once it is back in stock. Lemon gives us a fresh angle for people who already know the brand.
phases.creative: Add ads featuring the second flavor and any strong reviews we have collected. Test a repeat-purchase or bundle message.
phases.budget: Scale spend in line with results. We only go up while the return holds.
phases.what_success_looks_like: A steady, profitable core plus a second product live and a clearer picture of what a repeat customer is worth.

estimates.label: Starting budget
estimates.basis: Enough to get roughly 30 to 40 clicks a day at typical costs in this category, which is the floor for learning which ad and audience actually work.

estimates.label: Revenue we would aim to add
estimates.value: I would not commit to a hard number on the call
estimates.basis: With an order value that could be as low as $11 and no proof yet that any ad converts, a revenue promise would be a guess. Once we know their real average order value and whether they have repeat buyers, I can size this honestly.

assumptions: The average order value is more than $11. If most orders really are a single $11 bottle, paid ads will be hard to make profitable and the plan changes. We need the real number.
The Fresh Lemon Ice comes back in stock during the 90 days. If not, phase 3 stays on the mint product only.
Eddie can get us at least a handful of real reviews to use, since the site shows almost none right now.
Their margin on an $11 to $17 bottle leaves room to pay for ads. We must confirm this early.

questions.question: Are you running any paid ads right now, on Meta or anywhere else?
questions.listening_for: I found no Meta ads live. If they say none, we are starting from zero, which is fine. If they say yes, ask where, because something may have stopped and I could not see it.

questions.question: Has anyone tried Meta ads for you before, in-house or another agency? What happened?
questions.listening_for: Past failure, burned budget, or bad experience. If they got burned, they will be cautious about spend and we lead with the small, prove-it-first plan.

questions.question: What is your actual average order value, and do people tend to buy more than one bottle or come back to reorder?
questions.listening_for: This is the make-or-break number. If most orders are one $11 bottle with no repeat buying, paid ads are tough. If people buy the 64oz or reorder, the math opens up fast.

questions.question: Roughly what does it cost you to make and ship a bottle, so we know what room we have to spend on ads?
questions.listening_for: Whether the margin can carry paid traffic. Thin margin on a cheap product is a real risk. Do not push spend until you hear this.

questions.question: What would a good result in the first 90 days look like to you?
questions.listening_for: Are their hopes realistic. If they expect huge scale in month one, reset gently to the prove-it-first plan.

questions.question: When someone lands on your product page, how often do they actually buy? Do you know your rough conversion rate?
questions.listening_for: If traffic already converts, the fix is getting more traffic. If it does not, we fix the page before spending big. Do not guess a number back at them.

questions.question: I noticed there are almost no reviews on your site. Do you have reviews collected somewhere else we could use?
questions.listening_for: Any stash of testimonials, emails, or dental pro endorsements. Ads without social proof are much harder. This directly shapes the creative.

questions.question: The Fresh Lemon Ice is showing sold out. When is it coming back, and is the mint fully in stock at all sizes?
questions.listening_for: Stock reliability. We cannot advertise what we cannot ship. This decides which product leads and when the second one joins.

questions.question: Which of your use cases sells best - piercings, dental recovery, canker sores, or everyday use?
questions.listening_for: Their own read on the strongest angle. Confirms or challenges my plan to lead with the healing and recovery message.

questions.question: Who is your customer, mostly? Everyday shoppers, people with a specific problem, or dentists buying for patients?
questions.listening_for: If dental pros are a big buyer, that is a whole separate angle and possibly a different funnel.

questions.question: When people look for a mouthwash for canker sores, piercings, or dry mouth, do you show up in Google search today?
questions.listening_for: These are things people clearly type into Google, so there is a real search opening here. If they rank poorly or run no Google ads, that is an honest add-on later, not a first-call pitch.

questions.question: You have a big Knowledge Center and clinical studies on the site. Is that bringing you any search traffic now?
questions.listening_for: They already have content most brands lack. If it is not ranking, SEO is a genuine opportunity. Only raise this if they seem open, after the ads plan lands.

objections.objection: We have been fine for 20 years without paid ads. Why start now?
objections.answer: That long track record is exactly why this is low risk. You already have a proven product people repurchase. Right now no one new is discovering you through Meta. We would start small, prove it works on one product, and only spend more once the sales are real.

objections.objection: Our mouthwash is only about $11. Can ads even be profitable on that?
objections.answer: That is the first thing I want to check with you, and I will not pretend to know it yet. If most people buy a single $11 bottle, it is harder. But you also sell 16oz and 64oz sizes, and if people reorder, the real value of a customer is much higher than one bottle. Once I know your true average order value and repeat rate, I will tell you straight if this makes sense.

objections.objection: We do not have many reviews to show. Won't that hurt the ads?
objections.answer: It does make it harder, and I want to be honest about that. But you have real customer quotes, dental professional trust, and a patented formula that is 20 years old. That is strong proof we can build ads around. Getting even a handful of reviews live would help a lot, and I can help you plan that.

objections.objection: We only wanted the creative. We are not sure about a full campaign.
objections.answer: Totally fair, and the playbook and mockups are yours to keep either way. The reason I suggest running them is that ad creative only shows its value once real money is behind it against the right audience. I would rather start tiny and let the results decide than ask you to commit big up front.

red_flags.flag: Listed prices are very low, from $11 up.
red_flags.why_it_matters: If the typical order is a single cheap bottle, there may not be enough margin to pay for ads and still profit. We must confirm the real average order value and repeat rate before pushing spend.

red_flags.flag: No reviews visible anywhere on the site.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Social proof is one of the biggest drivers of whether an ad converts. Starting with almost none makes the first month harder and means we need to collect reviews fast.

red_flags.flag: The Fresh Lemon Ice product is sold out.
red_flags.why_it_matters: We cannot advertise a product we cannot ship. This forces us to lead with the mint and confirm stock reliability before scaling.

red_flags.flag: It is a health and oral care product with healing and recovery claims.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Meta restricts health claims. We must avoid promising to cure canker sores, gum disease, or treat chemo side effects. The playbook already lists what we cannot say, and we must stick to it or ads get rejected.

red_flags.flag: They emailed from two different addresses (eddie@h2ocean.com and h2oceaned@gmail.com).
red_flags.why_it_matters: Minor, but worth noting so Kyle uses the right contact and knows he is talking to the same person, Eddie.

other_channel_openings: Google Search ads: people actively type things like mouthwash for canker sores, piercing aftercare rinse, and dry mouth mouthwash. That is real demand we could capture. Raise it after the Meta plan lands.
SEO: they already have a large Knowledge Center, clinical studies, and articles. Most brands do not have this content. If it is not ranking, there is a genuine long-term search opportunity here.
